paragraphs: The Political Economy of Neoliberal Narratives ∗ Timothy Besley LSE Adam Brzezinski LSE November 28, 2024 Abstract This paper develops a canonical approach to incorporating narratives into standard models of political competition. Politicians supply narratives to win elections, and voters demand narratives that suit their interests, based on motivated reasoning. We apply this framework to study the rise of ne- oliberal narratives that emphasise the trickle-down benefits of deregulation. We provide insights into why neoliberalism gained traction following the 1970s oil shocks, and how it evolved into a new consensus narrative, some- times called the Third Way. Our framework shows how temporary eco- nomic shocks can lead to a narrative dynamic that permanently alters policy choices.

∗ We are grateful to Roland B´enabou, Jonathan Bendor, Steve Callander, Paul Collier, Matt Gentzkow, Larry Kramer, Gilat Levy, Itzhak Rasooly, Andrei Shleifer, Steven Teles, David Tuckett, Stephane Wolton and a number of seminar participants at LSE, King’s College, Stanford, University of Nottingham, and the IOG-BFI conference for helpful comments and insights.

Narratives that are used for interpreting how the world works have long been seen as crucial for shaping policy debates. Discussions of economic policy problems are frequently communicated to citizens via narratives in order to influence their voting behavior and thereby to affect policy choices. Yet, until recently, policy narratives were largely ignored by theoretical frameworks in political economy despite increasing interest in their role among economists (e.g., Shiller, 2017). This paper develops a canonical approach for bringing narratives into standard models of political competition (e.g., Downs, 1957; Persson and Tabellini, 2002), following an emerging literature that models narratives as subjective causal models (e.g., Eliaz and Spiegler, 2020; Levy et al., 2022; Brzezinski, 2023). In our frame- work, voters demand narratives to make sense of the world and to inform their policy preferences. They look at the set of available narratives, adopting the one that they find most appealing, based on motivated reasoning. Politicians supply narratives in political campaigns, communicating them strategically to influence how voters behave in order to gain an electoral advantage. The set of narratives that voters adopt influences which policy platforms are ex-post credible. In polit- ical equilibrium, politicians will deploy new narratives only if they lead to policy convictions that improve their electoral chances. Our framework allows us to explore a new dimension of policy disagreement driven by narrative polarization, where groups of citizens endogenously choose competing causal models of how policy affects economic outcomes. This enriches political disagreement beyond standard economic interests and allows us to capture Keynes’ famous view expressed in the concluding paragraph of his General Theory of Employment, Interest, and Money (Keynes, 1936) that “the power of vested interests is vastly exaggerated compared with the gradual encroachment of ideas. . . sooner or later, it is ideas, not vested interests, which are dangerous for good or evil.” And it chimes with the views of narrative psychologists that the human mind absorbs narratives to supplement logic and evidence-based thinking (e.g., Johnson et al., 2020). To illustrate the utility of the framework, we employ it to explore the rise of a particular policy narrative, often referred to as neoliberalism , a worldview that emphasises the virtues of free markets. Neoliberalism is now often used as a pejorative term, though it did not originate as such; indeed, some of its leading proponents proudly self-identified as neoliberals (Burgin, 2012). Among these was Milton Friedman, who wrote that “neo-liberalism offers a real hope of a better future, a hope that is already a strong cross-current of opinion and that is capable of capturing the enthusiasm of men of good-will everywhere, and thereby becoming the major current of opinion” (Friedman, 1951, p. 4). A contemporaneous example 1 of self-identification with neoliberalism is the Adam Smith Institute in the UK, which in 2016 started to use the term to describe its policy views. 1 In this paper, we refrain from taking a normative stance for or against policies referred to as neoliberal. Instead, we analyse the conditions under which neoliberal narratives thrive. 2 This, we argue, depends on two factors: whether neoliberal narratives are demanded by voters, and whether they are supplied by parties. On the demand-side, neoliberal narratives will only be popular if they give hope of a better future to (some) voters, as suggested in the quote by Friedman above. On the supply side, neoliberal narratives will be deployed by parties if that leads to an electoral advantage, which only happens under specific conditions. In order to investigate the political success of neoliberalism, our framework gives a precise definition of the term. Specifically, neoliberal narratives are causal policy models with two key features: (i) they highlight the trickle-down benefits of policies which harm poorer individuals, arguing that losses will be more than offset by gains accruing from a larger size of the economy; and (ii) they question the ability of government to produce public goods efficiently. The intellectual history of neoliberal thought stretches back to von Mises, Hayek, and other Austrian economists, amidst the fall of the Austro-Hungarian empire after WWI (Slobodian, 2018; Burgin, 2012). They became common cur- rency with the rise of the Chicago School, notably under the influence of Milton Friedman. Yet, for the most part, these ideas remained on the fringe of policy thinking, until they became influential following the oil crises of the 1970s and the subsequent rise to power of Margaret Thatcher and Ronald Reagan (Sted- man Jones, 2014). Neoliberalism fractured a policy consensus that had emerged following the Great Depression and World War II, one that argued for a wider role for govern- ment in the form of demand management and regulation of markets. This  post-war consensus travels under a variety of names. In the US, Gerstle (2022) refers to it as “the New Deal Order”. In the UK, it is associated with the term “Butskellism”, coined by The Economist magazine in 1954 to describe the convergence of views across the Labour and Conservative parties (see Harrison, 1999). Under this con- sensus, policies of parties from the left and right frequently resembled each other. In the US, Eisenhower, a Republican president, embraced most of the policy plat- forms of Franklin D. Roosevelt. In the UK, the Conservative party switched to supporting the National Health Service in the same time period. While there were dissenters in both cases, policy continuity remained the norm as adminstrations turned over. 1 See https://www.adamsmith.org/blog/coming-out-as-neoliberals . 2 A related paper is B´enabou (2008), which offers a model of different possible ideologies about the scope of state intervention versus free markets.

2 That the rise of Margaret Thatcher and Ronald Reagan came with new policy narratives is hard to dispute. An iconic example of the shift in emphasis can be found in Ronald Reagan’s inaugural address in 1981 (Reagan, 1981): We invented the assembly line and mass production, but punitive tax policies and excessive and unnecessary regulations plus government borrowing have stifled our ability to update plant and equipment. When capital investment is made, it’s too often for some unproductive alterations demanded by government to meet various of its regulations. Reagan’s shift away from interventionism was a clear departure from views that had dominated policy-thinking in preceding decades. Moreover, the associated policies gained support from voters with both Reagan and Thatcher going on to be re-elected, in the latter case twice. Neoliberalism also fared well elsewhere in the same time period and the “Washington Consensus” became an established blueprint for policy reforms around the world. Policy changes such as the shift away from the post-war consensus towards neoliberalism do not come out of thin air; in democracies they are the product of a competitive political process. In standard models of political competition where voters are assumed to have a common understanding of how the economy works, policy shifts are caused by new evidence or a change in economic interests. As such approaches assume away the possibility that citizens have different beliefs about how policies work, they also cannot explain polarization in beliefs of the kind that dominated politics in the US and UK in the 1980s. In contrast to this standard approach, our framework allows citizens to choose among competing narratives that combine causal parameters across the different dimensions in which policy affects outcomes into a coherent world view. Citizens choose the narratives that maximise their expected utility, a process of motivated reasoning that can be thought of as occurring subconsciously. Put simply, citizens believe things because they are convenient rather than because they are evidence- based, an idea that has long been accepted by psychologists (see Kunda, 1990), but is relatively new in economic theory (see B´enabou, 2015; B´enabou and Tirole, 2016). This approach allows us to investigate situations where citizens disagree because of the subjective causal model that they use rather than because of differ- ences in evidence. In our application to neoliberalism, this approach can shed light on why some groups of citizens were convinced of the neoliberal approach while others were not, how this led to polarization in the policies of parties, and why a new cross-party consensus around the merits of deregulation eventually formed. Beyond shedding light on the rise of neoliberalism, our framework offers some more general insights into how narratives shape policy. A central take-away is that temporary economic shocks can lead to a narrative dynamic that perma- nently shifts policy equilibria. This is because economic shocks can incentivize 3 parties to deploy new narratives that otherwise would not have been electorally advantageous. These narratives become part of the public discourse, preventing the return to a previous policy equilibrium, even after the economy recovers. We also show that “dominated” narratives, that are unanimously less preferred to an- other narrative, can become part of a political equilibrium. Parties may find it optimal to supply dominated narratives precisely because these will be believed by some, but not all, citizens. Such narrative polarization feeds into policy po- larization, giving one party a temporary electoral advantage, until another party deploys a narrative that leads to a new consensus. Our framework shows why polarizing narratives that give one party an electoral advantage, and consensus narratives which eliminate that advantage, can alternate dynamically. Our model builds on an emerging literature in political economy that inves- tigates competition in narratives. 3 There is now an established convention in economic theory of defining narratives as subjective causal models. 4 However, the way in which competition in narratives is modelled, and how narratives are as- sumed to relate to available data, varies widely. For example, Eliaz and Spiegler (2020) assume that narratives may incorrectly specify the causal chain between variables, but that, conditional on that causal chain, beliefs are assumed to fit available data (see also Eliaz et al., 2022). Schwartzstein and Sunderam (2021) posit that the most persuasive causal models are those that most closely fit the data to be explained, an idea also taken up by Aina (2023). And Levy et al. (2022) develop a model where voters have exogenously given narratives, some of which are incorrect; conditional on these narratives, voters update beliefs consistently with the available data. In our framework, motivated reasoning rather than data disciplines the narra- tives that voters adopt. Such reasoning is a well-established idea in psychology (Kunda, 1990; Haidt, 2012; Van der Linden, 2023) and has been adopted in formal models in political economy (B´enabou, 2008, 2015; Brzezinski, 2023; Little, 2019). The idea of motivated reasoning connects to an older tradition in economic theory that questions Bayesian belief formation (see Ortoleva, 2022, for a recent review). Previous approaches have highlighted the role of emotions against outside groups (Glaeser, 2005; Alesina and Stantcheva, 2020), social networks and group iden- tity (Murphy and Shleifer, 2004; Shayo, 2009; Bonomi et al., 2021), and ideologies (B´enabou, 2008; Izzo et al., 2023). Although our purpose is to build a theoretical framework, it is closely allied to an emerging empirical literature that investigates narratives and related concepts. Several tools have been developed to analyse text in political speeches (Gentzkow 3 This literature complements the widespread approach of Bayesian persuasion models (Ka- menica and Gentzkow, 2011; Kamenica, 2019). 4 Note, however, that some papers define narratives differently (e.g. B´enabou et al., 2018).

4 et al., 2019; Ash et al., 2024). Moreover, there is increasing recognition that beliefs are not solely data-driven, but can be influenced by plot lines in soap operas (La Ferrara et al., 2012), reality shows (Kearney and Levine, 2015), divergent narratives in opinion programs on TV (Bursztyn et al., 2023), videos of immigrant life experience (Alesina et al., 2023), light entertainment broadcasting (Durante et al., 2019), and plays (Hoff et al., 2021). The remainder of the paper proceeds as follows. Section 2 introduces our core model, which builds on standard approaches in political economy, but introduces the concept of narratives as alternative causal models of how policy affects out- comes. Section 3 solves a static model for a choice between two narratives: the post-war consensus and neoliberalism. Section 4 introduces a wider range of nar- ratives and extends the model dynamically, so that parties can choose whether to propagate new narratives based on the way that they affect their electoral prospects. We summarise our insights and directions for future research in section 5. The Appendix contains the proofs and other supporting material.

2.1 Basics We begin by outlining the core model that makes precise the content of different political narratives and use it to explore the role of narratives in political compe- tition. Private incomes and deregulation The economy is modelled following Besley and Persson (2023). There are two types of citizens differentiated by their level of income indexed by K ∈{ L, H } , with private pre-tax incomes α L and α H , where α L < α H . The proportion of type L citizens is denoted by µ . There are two policy dimensions, redistribution and regulation, where the latter affects the pre- tax distribution of income. Specifically, there is an income tax t ∈ [ t, t ] which is used to provide public services on an equal per capita basis, denoted by G . The regulation policy is denoted by x ∈ [ x, x ], where higher x corresponds to de regulation. The real income of group K as a function of regulation is y K ( x ;  ψ, λ ) =  α K +  λ K ( x ;  ψ, λ ) , (1) where λ K ( x ;  ψ, λ ) =  λx for K =  H − ψλx for K =  L. The rich always get higher income with deregulation while the effect on the poor is the opposite. The parameter λ ≥ 0 measures the importance of deregulation 5 in shaping incomes, while the parameter ψ ≥ 0 captures the negative distributive effect on the poor. Aggregate income is denoted by Y  ( x ;  ψ, λ ) = (1  − µ )  y H ( x ;  ψ, λ ) +  µy L ( x ;  ψ, λ ) = α  +  λx (1  − µ  − ψµ ) , where  α  ≡ (1  − µ )  α H + µα L . Aggregate income is increasing in  x  if  ψ <  (1 − µ ) /µ , i.e. if the negative distributive effect is small enough. We call this inequality the trickle-down condition ; if it holds, then part of the benefits of deregulation to the rich will trickle down in the shape of higher public good provision to the poor. Public goods and taxation The government finances the public good G via taxes. Total public goods provision satisfies  G  =  γtY  ( x ;  ψ, λ ) where  γ ≥ 0 captures the efficiency and/or valuation of public goods. If γ is low, then the production of public goods is inefficient, either because taxes cannot be collected without a deadweight loss, or because public goods are not highly valued. The opposite is true for high γ . Individuals receive utility from their private post-tax income as well as the provision of public goods G . Utility for an individual from income group K is given by: u K ( t, x ;  ψ, λ, γ ) = (1  − t ) y K ( x ;  ψ, λ ) +  γtY  ( x ;  ψ, λ ) . (2) Narratives The three causal parameters λ, ψ and γ influence how deregulation x affects utility. Beliefs about these parameters will be endogenously determined through the adoption of policy narratives: subjective causal models that describe how policies influence outcomes. Denote the finite sets of possible causal parameters as: λ ∈ Λ , ψ ∈ Ψ and γ ∈ Γ. A  narrative,  ω , combines these parameters into a subjective causal model, represented as a probability distribution over the set T = Λ × Ψ × Γ. Now let Ωbe the set of possible narratives and P ω  ( λ, ψ, γ ) be the weight that narra- tive ω ∈ Ωattaches to parameter triple { λ, ψ, γ } . For each ω , we suppose that P { λ,ψ,γ }∈T P ω  ( λ, ψ, γ ) = 1. These weights could be thought of as probabilities over a state space, or as weights assigned to different scenarios based on listening to different accounts of how the world works. Let U K ( t, x, ω ) = X { ψ,λ,γ }∈T P ω  ( ψ, λ, γ )  u K ( t, x ;  ψ, λ, γ ) (3) be the expected utility from narrative ω and policies ( t, x ). We will focus on situations where all citizens of the same type use the same narrative. However, this does not preclude citizens of different types adopting different narratives.

6 Figure 1: A Conviction Narrative Hence, let { ω L , ω H } be the narratives that citizens of each corresponding income level adopt. We call ω a conviction narrative if it specifies a degenerate distribution over T  , i.e. one that assigns all of the weight to a triple of parameters  { λ, ψ, γ }  so that P ω  ( λ, ψ, γ ) = 1. 5 Let C ⊆ Ω, be the set of conviction narratives. We assume that for any parameter triple { λ, ψ, γ } that is in the support of some narrative  ω , there exists a conviction narrative  ν which assigns full weight to that triple. In other words, if for a triple ( λ, ψ, γ ) we have  P ω ( λ, ψ, γ )  >  0 for some narrative ω , then there also exists a conviction narrative ν with P ν ( λ, ψ, γ ) = 1. It is straightforward to illustrate a conviction narrative in our model as a directed acyclical graph (DAG) as shown in Figure 1. The effect of  x  on the incomes of high- and low-income citizens is determined by λ and ( λ, ψ ), respectively. The follow-on effect of average incomes onto the provision of public goods is determined by γ . This shows a similarity to the representation of narratives as DAGs in Eliaz and Spiegler (2020), with a difference in the dimension in which the narratives vary. In Eliaz and Spiegler (2020), narratives vary in the causal ordering of variables; conditional on an order, however, parameters that describe causal relationships are 5 We borrow the term “conviction narrative” from Tuckett and Nikolic (2017) and Johnson et al. (2020). Tuckett and Nikolic (2017, p. 502) suggest that “conviction narratives enable actors to draw on the information, beliefs, causal models, and rules of thumb situated in their social context to identify opportunities worth acting on, to simulate the future outcome of the actions by means of which they plan to achieve those opportunities, and to feel sufficiently convinced about the anticipated outcomes to act.” 7 consistent with the data. In contrast, in our approach there is no disagreement about the direction of causation, and instead the disagreement is in the size of the relevant parameters. Parties Policy is determined in political equilibrium by competition between two parties that represent each group. Parties are denoted by J ∈{ L, H } and choose deregulation x J ∈ [ x, x ] and a tax t J ∈ [ t, t ]. The two parties are staffed by low- and high- income citizen-candidates, respectively, who cannot commit to policies ex-ante. Thus the only incentive compatible policies are those that maximise the utility of their own income type. To chime with standard party classifications in political science, we will refer to the high income party as the Right and the low income party as the Left . Party platforms comprise tax rates, { t L , t H } , and regulations, { x L , x R } which since they are chosen after the election are given by: { ˆ t J ,  ˆ x J }  = arg max t,x U  J  � t, x,  ˆ ω J  , (4) where ˆ ω J is the equilibrium narrative of citizens of income group  J , discussed fur- ther below. In other words, policies of party J are chosen to maximise ex-post utility of citizens from the matching income group, given the narrative adopted by these citizens. Hence, as we discuss further below, the only strategic element in policy-making is via shifting the narrative that citizens adopt. If multiple reg- ulation policies solve the above, we assume that ˆ x H = x , ˆ t H = t , ˆ x L = x and ˆ t L =  t . 6 Voting We use a standard probabilistic voting model as in Persson and Tabellini (2002) with both an idiosyncratic  ϵ  pro-party  H shock that is uniformly distributed on  − 1 2 σ , 1 2 σ  and an aggregate pro-party  H shock,  η , that is uniformly distributed on  − 1 2 κ , 1 2 κ  . Then a voter of type K will vote for party H if U K ( ˆ t H ,  ˆ x H ; ˆ ω K ) +  ϵ  +  η ≥ U K ( ˆ t L ,  ˆ x L ; ˆ ω K ) . (5) Thus the vote share of party H is S H = 1 2 +  σ  η  +  µ ∆ U L  � ˆ t H ,  ˆ x H , ˆ t L ,  ˆ x L ; ˆ ω L  + (1  − µ ) ∆ U H  � ˆ t H ,  ˆ x H , ˆ t L ,  ˆ x L ; ˆ ω H  where ∆ U K  � ˆ t H ,  ˆ x H , ˆ t L ,  ˆ x L ; ˆ ω K  =  U K ( ˆ t H ,  ˆ x H ; ˆ ω K )  − U K ( ˆ t L ,  ˆ x L ; ˆ ω K ). The voting system is majoritarian and the party with half the votes wins the election. So the 6 We specify this for completeness, but the assumption does not play an important role in analysis and could be replaced by other assumptions.

8 winning probability for party H is given by ˆ π � ˆ t H ,  ˆ x H ,  ˆ t L ,  ˆ x L ; ˆ ω L ,  ˆ ω H  (6) = 1 2 +  κ  µ ∆ U L  � ˆ t H ,  ˆ x H , ˆ t L ,  ˆ x L ; ˆ ω L  + (1  − µ ) ∆ U H  � ˆ t H ,  ˆ x H , ˆ t L ,  ˆ x L ; ˆ ω H  which we will assume has an interior solution (i.e. κ is low enough). Narrative Adoption Beliefs are motivated and maximise expected utility: ˆ ω K = arg max ω ∈ Ω ˆ πU  K  � ˆ t H ,  ˆ x H , ω  + (1  − ˆ π )  U K  � ˆ t L ,  ˆ x L , ω  (7) where they take the political equilibrium   ˆ π, ˆ t H ,  ˆ x H , ˆ t L ,  ˆ x L as given. This describes one non-standard feature of the model: citizens “choose” their preferred narrative, which will influence their policy preferences and thereby the equilibrium policies of parties. As in other models of motivated reasoning, such as B´enabou (2015), this process of narrative adoption can be thought of as happening subconsciously. When this narrative adoption happens as part of a political equi- librium, it will be affected by what citizens believe will be the policy outcomes. And this, in turn, may depend on the narrative choices they think that others will make. Our approach to political equilibrium demands that this happens in a consistent way, as we describe next.
Equilibrium Structure The timing of the model is as follows:
1. Citizens choose a narrative from the available set. 2. Citizens vote. 3. The winning party implements policy. 4. Payoffs are realized.

A political equilibrium comprises a pair of narratives   ˆ ω L ,  ˆ ω H adopted by each group that satisfy (7); a set of policy choices   ˆ t J ,  ˆ x J for J ∈{ H, L } that satisfy (4); and a winning probability ˆ π that satisfies (6).

In this section, we use the framework of the previous section to study neoliberal narratives. The specificity of our modelling approach makes it possible to state precisely what we mean by “neoliberalism”, a term that is often only vaguely defined. We will characterise the conditions for a narrative consensus or polariza- tion across groups of citizens and show how the success of the neoliberal narrative depends on the state of the economy.

3.1 Basics To simplify the analysis, we assume that there is common tax policy across par- ties, i.e. t  =  t  =  t . 7 Without loss of generality, we focus exclusively on conviction narratives  C that put all of their weight on one particular parameter vector. Specif- ically: Proposition 1. For any political equilibrium, there exists an equilibrium with the same policy outcomes where citizens adopt conviction narratives. The reason for this is that if citizens adopt a non-conviction narrative in equi- librium, they must be indifferent between all parameter triplets that they assign weight to. For each group J , at least one of these triplets will be consistent with the original equilibrium policies { ˆ x J } . Given this, we will focus exclusively on conviction narratives ν ∈C in the sequel, and we will denote the equilibrium conviction narratives by { ˆ ν L ,  ˆ ν H } . From now on, we will also focus on the case where causal parameters that make up a narrative have binary values, i.e. λ ∈   λ, λ , ψ ∈   ψ, ψ and γ ∈   γ, γ , where upper-bars denote strictly larger values than lower-bars. This means that there are eight possible conviction narratives. However, to start with, we focus on two of these. The first we call the post-war consensus narrative : ν c =  { λ c , ψ c , γ c }  =   λ, ψ, γ , (8) and the second the neoliberal narrative: ν n =  { λ n , ψ n , γ n }  =   λ, ψ, γ . (9) Comparing these two narratives, observe that (i)  λ n > λ c so that the neoliberal narrative proposes that deregulation has a larger impact on incomes than does the post-war consensus narrative, (ii)  ψ n < ψ c so that the post-war narrative proposes that deregulation has a larger direct negative impact on low income individuals 7 We relax this assumption in Supplemental Appendix S.

10 and (iii)  γ n < γ c so that the neoliberal narrative views the state as less efficient and public goods as less important than the post-war consensus narrative. Although stylized, we believe that these definitions capture core elements of these views and, in particular, give a precise interpretation of terms that are usually left vague. We will also work with some parametric restrictions. The first ensures that narrative divergence implies policy divergence, which gives narrative choice some bite in our framework: 8 tγ (1  − µ (1 +  ψ )) ≥ ψ (1  − t ) and (10) (1  − t ) ≤ tγ ( µ (1 +  ψ )  − 1) .

These conditions imply that the trickledown condition holds for the neoliberal narrative, such that x is believed to increase aggregate income, but not for the post-war consensus narrative. Finally, we also focus on the case where the low income type suffers more from deregulation with the neoliberal narrative than the consensus narrative, i.e. λψ ≥ λψ .

We will now study citizen choice between the neoliberal narrative  ν n and the post- war consensus narrative  ν c ; in the next section, we will turn to the choice between all narratives. To study the political equilibrium with these two narratives, we work backwards. Policy Choices Given a pair of narrative choices,   ν L , ν H , the optimal regu- latory policy of party  J ∈{ L, H }  depends only on the narrative of their core type, i.e. solves x J � ν J  = arg max x ∈ [ x,x ]   u J  � t, x, ν J  . (11) Given condition (10), this implies that x J ( ν c ) = x and x J ( ν n ) = x for J ∈ { L, H } . In other words, either party implements deregulation if the citizens from their income class believe in neoliberalism, and implements regulation if they be- lieve in the post-war consensus. For simplicity of notation, since both parties offer the same policy for a narrative  ν ∈{ ν c , ν n } , from now on we drop the subscript J and write x ( ν ) to denote the policy that follows from narrative ν . 8 In particular, the condition ensures that any narrative that includes ψ ( ψ ) implies that deregulation (regulation) is ex-post preferred by both types.

11 Winning Probabilities Given this, the winning probability of party J using (6), as a function of narrative choices { ν L , ν H } , is π � ν L , ν H  = 1 2 +  κ  µ ∆ U L  � t, x � ν H  , t, x � ν L  + (1  − µ ) ∆ U H  � t, x � ν H  , t, x � ν L  (12) Narrative Choices To study narrative choices, it is useful to define for each in- come group  J ∈{ L, H } , the perceived gain from adopting the neoliberal narrative relative to the post-war consensus: E J  � x ( ν L ) , x ( ν H )  ≡ π � ν L , ν H   u J ( t, x ( ν H ) , ν n )  − u J ( t, x ( ν H ) , ν c )  +[1  − π � ν L , ν H  ]  u J ( t, x ( ν L ) , ν n )  − u J ( t, x ( ν L ) , ν c )  =  π � ν L , ν H   (1  − t )∆ y J  � x � ν H  +  t ∆ G � x � ν H  (13) +  1  − π � ν L , ν H   (1  − t )∆ y J  � x � ν L  +  t ∆ G � x � ν L  which consists of two main components denoted by ∆ y J ( x ) and ∆ G ( x ). The first component is the perceived difference in private income across the two narratives given by: ∆ y J ( x )  ≡ y J ( x ;  ψ, λ )  − y J ( x ;  ψ, λ ) , (14) The second component is the difference in public goods provision between the neoliberal narrative and the post-war consensus given by: ∆ G  ( x ) ≡ γY  ( x ;  ψ, λ )  − γY  ( x ;  ψ, λ ) = − α � γ  − γ  +  x  λγ � 1  − µ � 1 +  ψ  − λγ � 1  − µ � 1 +  ψ  (15) The first term in (15) is negative, representing the more pessimistic view of govern- ment efficiency under neoliberalism. The second term is positive given condition (10), due to the increase in aggregate income when the trickle-down condition holds; the neoliberal narrative is more optimistic about the scope for deregulation to increase aggregate output and hence public goods. If expression (13) is positive for an income group, then members of the group will choose to believe the neoliberal narrative; if it is negative, they will choose the post-war consensus narrative. Since policy depends on the beliefs of all cit- izens, second-order beliefs matter when assessing which narrative to believe in, as they influence the pair of narratives ( ν L , ν H ) which citizens use to evaluate E J ( x ( ν L ) , x ( ν H )). The sign and size of ∆ G ( x ) depends on mean income, α . The higher is mean income, the bigger is the perceived problem of government waste, and the less attractive is the neoliberal narrative. This parameter therefore plays a key role in determining which narrative equilibrium arises:
12 Proposition 2. There are three ranges of α with cutoff values α < α such that:

1. If and only if α ∈ ( α, α ) , there is an equilibrium with narrative and pol- icy divergence where the high (low) income citizens believe in the neoliberal narrative (post-war consensus narrative), and the high (low) income party delivers deregulation (regulation) if elected; 2. if  α  ≤ α  there is an equilibrium with full consensus on the neoliberal narrative and deregulation; 3. if α  ≥ α , there is an equilibrium with full consensus on the post-war consen- sus narrative and regulation.

Proposition 2 summarises how the state of the economy, α , influences the political equilibria. In particular, when  α  is very high or very low, the equilibrium will be one of narrative convergence across high- and low-income citizens, and policy convergence across parties; only in an intermediate range of α is there scope for narrative and policy divergence. The logic of the proposition can be understood in four steps. First, observe that if low-income citizens believe in the neoliberal narrative in equilibrium, then high-income citizens must as well. This is because high-income individuals get the same benefit as low-income individuals from the change in public output when believing in the neoliberal narrative. But they get a strictly larger private benefit. A similar logic implies that if the high-income citizens believe in the post-war consensus, then so must the low-income citizens. The only kind of divergent beliefs that are possible are therefore where the high-income citizens adopt the neoliberal narrative while the low income citizens adopt the post war consensus narrative. Second, consider what happens if α is very high. Then, the neoliberal narra- tive’s pessimistic view of government efficiency makes it unattractive even to high- income citizens. Specifically, equation (15) shows that compared to the post-war consensus narrative, the lower government efficiency supposed by the neoliberal narrative leads to a loss in public output by  − ( γ  − γ ) α . With very high  α , this loss is so large that it overshadows any private gains to high-income citizens stemming from the neoliberal narrative. Then  a fortiori,  the low income citizens also oppose the neoliberal narrative, and instead subscribe to the post-war consensus. Third, consider the case where α is very low. Now, pessimism about public good efficiency with neoliberal narratives does not have a strong negative effect on utility. In contrast, the potential gains in public output from deregulation via the trickle-down effect are large, captured through the second term in equation (15). For low enough α , this trickle-down effect is sufficient to persuade even the 13 low income individuals to believe in the neoliberal narrative. A fortiori, the high income individuals will as well. Fourth, note that for middling values of α we can have that the public output is seen as roughly equal with both the neoliberal and the post-war consensus nar- rative. In other words, the two terms from equation (15) roughly cancel each other out, so that ∆ G  ( x ) ≃ 0. In that case, only the private gains from deregulation matter in forming a narrative preference, leading high income individuals to sup- port the neoliberal narrative and low income individuals to support the post-war consensus. Multiple Equilibria? Proposition 2 showed that a polarized equilibrium exists when α ∈ ( α, α ). However, this equilibrium need not be unique. We now show that there can be multiple equilibria which are interesting because they illustrate how second-order beliefs matter when citizens are choosing which narrative to follow. Specifically, if citizens think that others of their type are going to adopt a narrative, then this shifts policy and can make it optimal for them to choose that narrative, making the narrative a “self-fufilling prophecy”. The details are explained in Appendix B; here, we focus only on the main intuition. The logic is best explained in terms of the properties of the E J functions described in equation (13) since these determine when it is optimal for any given type to believe in the neoliberal narrative given their policy expectations which, in turn, depend on the narrative equilibrium. Figure 2 illustrates this family of functions in order to bring out the logic of the multiple equilibria. It plots E J functions for one particular “ordering”, the details of which are discussed in Appendix section B. 9 These  E J functions slope downwards as a function of  α  since the payoff of believing in neoliberalism is lower when α is higher, as we discussed in reference to Proposition 2. If E J ( x ( ν L ) , x ( ν H )) is positive for a range of α , this implies that there is a positive payoff from believing the neoliberal narrative for citizens of group J , given their beliefs ( ν L , ν H ) about the narratives that citizens of each type adopt. Beliefs about the equilibrium narratives that will be adopted determine which of the E J functions individuals use to evaluate the gain from adopting the neoliberal narrative. For example, if it is believed that all high- and low-income individuals will adopt the neoliberal narrative, the relevant gain from adopting the neoliberal narrative is E L ( x ( ν n ) , x ( ν n )) for low-income citizens and E H ( x ( ν n ) , x ( ν n )) for high-income citizens. Higher-order beliefs about other citizens’ beliefs can lead to different equilibria in the α -ranges [ α, ζ ] and [ ζ, α ] in Figure 2. This is because such higher-order beliefs determine whether the gain from adopting the neoliberal narrative is pos- itive or negative. The vertical lines in the figure show that depending on the E J function used to evaluate payoffs, adopting the neoliberal narrative can either have 9 The Figure assumes that condition B.1 is satisfied.

14 Figure 2: Illustration of Multiple Equilibria a positive or a negative payoff. In other ranges of α , the payoffs are positive or negative regardless of higher-order beliefs, making the equilibrium unique. 10 3.3

In the Supplemental Appendix S, we discuss two extensions to the framework. First, we discuss results when both deregulation x and tax policies t are endoge- nous. We show conditions under which neoliberalism comes with a tax cut, leading to polarization in both deregulation and tax policies. The main take-away of en- dogenising tax rates is that, although things get somewhat more complicated, the same broad conclusions can be drawn as with fixed taxes. Divergence in equi- librium narratives and policies, which now include both taxation and regulation, occur for intermediate values of α . Second, we consider an alternative microfoun- dation of the trickle-down mechanism. The trickle-down mechanism proposed in the main model runs through the provision of public goods, which generates a feedback from the aggregate economy to private payoffs. An alternative way to conceptualise differences between the neoliberal and post-war consensus narrative would be to suppose that these capture differences in individualistic versus collec- tivist worldviews. 11 The appendix shows that such a conceptualisation would lead to equivalent insights. 10 For instance, in the range of α ∈ ( ζ, ζ ) , all E H functions are positive and all E L functions negative. It follows that there is a unique equilibrium in this range, where high-income citizens always adopt ν n and low-income citizens ν c . 11 This brings this aspect of narratives closer to that suggested in B´enabou et al. (2018).

So far, we have assumed that the set of available narratives is given exogenously. We now explore the possibility that narratives are propagated as part of a party’s electoral strategy. Since this leads to the set of available narratives evolving over time, we develop a dynamic (infinite horizon) version of the model where we allow political parties to propagate new narratives to determine the narrative set that citizens can choose from. In this dynamic version of the model, narrative supply and demand are de- termined by the strategic interaction between politicians, who control political parties, and voters. Narratives will only become available to voters if it is in the interest of parties to propagate them, and they will only gain traction if it is in the interest of citizens to believe in them. So, even though many narratives are possible, not all of them will become part of a political equilibrium. The analysis highlights different possible dynamic paths of narratives and poli- cies. In particular, we show that weaker economic conditions will give rise to the neoliberal narrative among high-income citizens and policy polarization across parties, before both parties converge on a new consensus narrative that stresses the merits of deregulation. Even temporary economic shocks can unleash this nar- rative dynamic and permanently change the policy equilibrium. The framework therefore exhibits a natural form of hysteresis since, once a narrative is propagated in response to an economic shock, it continues to shape policy equilibria even after the economy recovers.

The Narrative Space Since the three causal parameters λ, ψ and γ can each take a high or low value, there are eight possible narratives to consider. Among these is the post-war consensus narrative ν c and the neoliberal narrative ν n that we have already studied. In the equilibrium of the dynamic model, one additional narrative that we have not considered so far turns out to play an important role: ν 3 =  { λ, ψ, γ } (16) We refer to ν 3 as the “Third Way” narrative, following a term that has been used to describe the politics of Clinton, Blair, and other politicians from tradi- tionally centre-left parties who adopted a more pro-market stance in the 1990s (Giddens, 1998). 12 This is a form of “left-wing neoliberalism” that combines el- 12 Interestingly, Gerstle (2022) uses the term “Third Way” as a description of the New Deal narrative that emerged in the 1930s. The term was also used by Wilhelm R¨opke, one of the Presidents of the Mont Pelerin Society, to describe an economic order that combines market- based approaches with some role for state intervention (Burgin, 2012).

16 ements of the neoliberal narrative ν n and the post-war consensus narrative ν c . Specifically, the Third Way narrative ν 3 agrees with neoliberalism on the benefits of deregulation (via a low ψ and high λ ), and agrees with the post-war consensus on the efficiency and importance of government spending (via high γ ). Propagating Narratives In each period  t , each party decides whether to prop- agate a conviction narrative ν ∈C . We denote this decision by party J by I ν J ∈{ 0 ,  1 } , where 1 denotes propagating narrative ν . The cost of propagating a narrative is δ . We avoid the trivial cases where the cost is so high that parties never wish to propagate new narratives. 13 This cost can have a variety of interpre- tations. Most basically, it could be a cost of advertising. However, it could also be thought of as representing a potential loss of reputation for the party associated with advocating a new narrative. It could also represent internal fights within a party between ideologues and opportunists. The main state variable in the dynamic version of the model is denoted by σ t , which consists of the set of narratives that are available to voters. We fix the initial state to be  σ 0 =  { ν c } , i.e. only the post-war consensus narrative is available so there is no meaningful narrative choice. If one or more parties propagate an additional narrative ν , it will be heard by all voters with an (exogenously given) probability p ∈ (0 ,  1). If it is heard, it is added to the set σ t , after which it is available to all voters forever more, i.e. there is no memory loss. With probability 1  − p , the narrative does not “cut through” with voters in which case there is no change in σ t . The probability p can be thought of as representing the probability that a narrative can be phrased in a way as to be convincing or understood by voters. This could depend on whether a party leader has the right level of charisma or capacity for rhetoric in the framing of the narrative. Politicians like Reagan, Thatcher, Clinton and Blair were famous in their capacities to frame their versions of neoliberalism in ways that voters found appealing. Party Motivations When choosing whether to propagate narratives, parties are assumed to maximize their short-run, i.e. period t , payoff. Moreover, we assume that the party leadership has lexicographic preferences, caring first and foremost about winning the election net of communication costs δ , and then secondarily about maximizing the welfare of their core supporters. This permits the simplest dynamic extension with analysis of policy outcome conditional on winning being identical to the last section.
13 In particular, this requires δ < p (max { π,  1  − π } − 1 2 ), where π is defined in equation (18) below.
17 Timing Within each period of the game, the timing is as follows:
1. Parties choose whether to propagate new narratives. 2. Citizens choose a narrative from the available set. 3. Citizens vote. 4. The winning party implements policy. 5. Payoffs are realized.
Stages 2-5 of the model are as in the previous section, with the exception that the set of available narratives is potentially different to what has been discussed so far.

We look for a Markov Perfect Equilibrium . Within any period t , each party J ’s optimal choice of narrative propagation will be a function of the state variable: I ν J ( σ t ). In the case of multiple equilibria, we will select a polarizing equilibrium whenever it exists. 14 The Dynamic Path The following Proposition describes the different paths that are possible in the dynamic model. After stating it, we will discuss the underlying logic with the formal proof appearing in the Appendix. 15 Proposition 3. The dynamic equilibrium depends on the state of the economy represented by α : 1. When the economy is doing well ( α  ≥ α ), then in the unique Markov Perfect equilibrium, no party deploys a new narrative, and the state remains σ t = { ν c } . Along the entire dynamic path, there is convergence on regulation across both parties. 14 This is for convenience. However, the analysis yields similar results under alternative as- sumptions about equilibrium selection, including if we make the opposite assumption that we only pick a polarizing narrative if it is unique. 15 To simplify the analysis, we focus on the case that condition (B.1) holds, which leads to the ordering of E J functions as discussed in Section B and shown in Figure 2. The details can be found in Appendix Proposition B.1. If we did not assume condition (B.1), our analysis would be very similar, but more taxonomic, as more cases would need to be considered in terms of the order of E J functions.

18 2. When the economy is doing less well ( α  ∈ (˜ α, α ) , with cutoff ˜ α < α ), then in any Markov Perfect equilibrium, along the equilibrium path one party deploys the neoliberal narrative ν n with positive probability, while the other deploys the Third Way narrative  ν 3 with positive probability. No other narratives are deployed. There are two possibilities: (a) the state first changes to { ν c , ν n } , leading to narrative divergence where only high income citizens believe in the neoliberal narrative as well as policy divergence where only the high income party supports deregula- tion. Thereafter, the state converges to the absorbing state { ν c , ν n , ν 3 } , where both parties support deregulation. (b) the state changes directly to the absorbing state { ν c , ν 3 } or { ν c , ν n , ν 3 } , where both parties support deregulation. This result distinguishes two cases depending on the state of the economy. Case 1 parallels our finding from the static model where there was convergence on the post-war consensus. However, this was only in comparison to the neoliberal narrative being available; this result shows that this is a global result even allowing for the set of all possible narratives to be propagated by parties. In this case, the economy will maintain a consensus regulation policy. The more interesting possibility is Case 2, when the economy is doing less well as represented by lower α . Now the Proposition says that one party will propagate the neoliberal narrative, and the other the Third Way narrative. Eventually, the state will converge to one where the Third Way narrative is successfully deployed, all citizens believe in it, and there is convergence on deregulation across both parties. However, before that happens, there is potential for the neoliberal narrative to generate polarization in citizen narratives and party policies.

To explain the logic of this result, recall that in the initial state σ 0 where only the post-war consensus narrative, ν c , is available, then (trivially) all voters believe in the merits of regulation, and there is no scope for cross-party policy difference. Thus ν c is a consensus narrative: all citizens believe in it, and hence both parties offer the same regulation policy x . As there is also no differentiation in tax policies, both parties are indistinguishable by voters in their policy stance. Hence, neither party has an electoral advantage, and each wins with probability one-half. The only way that a party can gain an electoral advantage is to deploy a polarizing narrative , i.e. one that will be believed by some, but not all, citizens. But for such a narrative to gain traction with voters, it has to be attractive enough to convince one income group, but not too attractive to convince both. Starting with the post-war consensus where  ν c is adopted by all citizens, only the neoliberal narrative  ν n can be polarizing for  α > ˜ α . We show in the proof of the Proposition, 19 that the remaining narratives cannot be polarizing either because they are too unattractive for any citizen to believe in them or because they are too attractive and would convince everyone. 16 The multi-dimensionality of the parameter space plays an important role in this result. Thus, the neoliberal narrative can create polarization because it dis- agrees with the post-war consensus on two dimensions. Disagreement with ν c on the trickle-down effect through a different assessment of  ψ implies policy disagree- ment. Furthermore, disagreement on government efficiency through a different γ creates an additional cleavage between income groups, increasing the potential for polarization. It is not inevitable that neoliberalism is polarizing and therefore strategically attractive for a party. Proposition 2 stressed that this depends on the state of the economy α . This reasoning is familiar from the static model since we know that with high α , neoliberal pessimism in government efficiency is too costly for either income group to find it attractive relative to the post-war consensus. Specifically, there is a loss of − ( γ − γ ) α from adopting the neoliberal narrative, as can be seen in equation (15). Thus, a strong economy — high α — makes neoliberalism unattractive. Proposition 2 also shows that, starting from a high  α , the neoliberal narrative can only break the post-war consensus if there is a downturn in the economy—a decrease in  α . This corresponds to an established view that neoliberal narratives gained traction during the economic turbulence of the 1970s, an idea captured succinctly in the preface of Friedman (1982, p. xiii-xiv):

“Only a crisis - actual or perceived - produces real change. When that crisis occurs, the actions that are taken depend on the ideas that are lying around. That, I believe, is our basic function: to develop alternatives to existing policies, to keep them alive and available until the politically impossible becomes the politically inevitable.” He is pointing out that narratives can exist in the toolkit of intellectuals, but they only become part of the political process when the conditions are right. The neoliberal narrative was clearly known before economic turbulance hit in the 1970s but our model suggests that it was not going to gain traction even if a party deployed it in the benign economic circumstances that prevailed. 17 In contrast, 16 See the appendix for the proof to Proposition 3. It outlines some subtleties in the reasoning. We show that some narratives will not be employed because they achieve the same electoral outcomes while being less preferred by citizens. One other narrative is polarizing for a lower range of α < ˜ α which we do not consider here; however, it is strictly dominated by ν n from the citizens’ perspective and therefore does not change the long-run outcomes either. 17 Of course, we do not allow economic shocks to be a function of the policies in place and doubtless some proponents of neoliberalism blamed the turbulence of the 1970s on the policies that prevailed. However, the oil shocks of the 1970s were plausibly exogenous.

20 after a downturn that leads to α  ∈ (˜ α, α ), the neoliberal narrative ν n is attractive to high-income but not low-income citizens. It follows that one party will find it optimal to propagate the neoliberal narrative. After the neoliberal narrative has been successfully deployed, leading to policy polarization, the party that deployed the narrative gains an electoral advantage. Since elections are zero-sum games, this puts the opposing party at a disadvantage. To negate this disadvantage, the optimal strategy is to bring forth a new narrative that restores the political fortune of the party that loses from neoliberalism. This is done by deploying the Third Way narrative. What is special about the Third Way narrative is that it overcomes the neolib- eral polarization and creates a new consensus by combining the most optimistic elements from the other competing narratives. It has neoliberal optimism about the value of deregulation as embodied in high λ and low ψ . It has post-war con- sensus optimism in its views about the effectiveness of government in the form of high γ . It realises that the Achilles heel of politicians like Reagan and Thatcher was their pessimism about the role of the state. After the Third Way narrative is successfully deployed, as it was by Clinton, Obama and Blair, it creates a new consensus on deregulation. With both parties converging onto the same policy, voters are again indifferent between parties, and no party benefits from an elec- toral advantage over the post-war consensus even though the underlying regulation policies are different. The results illustrate an interesting feature of dynamic political competition with narratives. Starting from a consensus, the neoliberal narrative is deployed along the equilibrium path even though, from the perspective of citizens, it is dominated by the Third Way narrative. Propagating neoliberalism is attractive for a party precisely because it is polarizing and the Third Way is electorally useful only when it is used to counter it in order to build a new consensus. Thus, neoliberalism can be thought of as a disruptive narrative to break a consensus. 18 For the same reason that the Third Way narrative can combat existing neolib- eral polarization, it can also preempt it. Whether or not it pays off for a party to try to preemptively propagate the Third Way narrative depends on the prob- ability of success of narratives, captured through the parameter p , as well as the costs of propagation, captured through the parameter δ . How these parameters exactly feed into the dynamic equilibrium depends on which party benefits from the polarized neoliberal equilibrium. When will Neoliberalism be propagated by right-wing parties? Proposi- tion 3 does not say  which party will propagate the neoliberal narrative. Given that parties are assumed to care about winning this is not a matter of party ideology. 18 Narrative competition therefore has some features that are familiar from models of creative destruction (Aghion and Howitt, 1992).

21 Moreover, the policy choice that any party makes simply follows what is optimal for the group that they represent. In reality, we know that parties of the political right, the Republicans in the US and the Tories in the UK, were the vanguard of neoliberalism. We now explore when our model predicts that this will be the case. The key condition that is needed for it to be the high income party to propagate the neoliberal narrative is: λ λ > µ 1  − µ (1  − t ) ψ  +  tγ ( µ (1 +  ψ )  − 1) (1  − t ) +  tγ (1  − µ (1 +  ψ )) (17) The right party receives additional votes from high-income voters who adopt the neoliberal narrative and hence prefer deregulation, but fewer votes from low-income voters who adhere to the post-war consensus narrative and support regulation. The gain from increased high-income votes exceeds the loss from low-income voters when (17) holds. Whenever λ is large enough relative to λ , party H gains from narrative polarization. In this polarized equilibrium where high income individuals adopt the neoliberal narrative, party H’s winning probability is: π ≡ 1 2 +  κ ( x  − x ))[(1  − µ ) λ � (1  − t ) +  tγ (1  − µ (1 +  ψ ))  (18) − µλ � (1  − t ) ψ  +  tγ ( µ (1 +  ψ )  − 1)  ] , which is greater than a half when (17) holds. Correspondingly, the left’s winning probability is then 1 − π . The left benefits from countering the neoliberal narrative in order to increase it’s winning chances back to one-half. However, whether it benefits from doing so pre-emptively rather than waiting for neoliberalism to arise depends on the probability that narrative deployment succeeds p and the cost of deploying narratives δ . This trade-off between benefits and costs is made precise in: Proposition 4. Suppose that condition (17) holds and that α ∈ (˜ α, α ) . Then there are three cases: 1. If δ ∈ ( p 2 ( π  − 0 . 5) , p ( π  − 0 . 5)) , in the unique equilibrium, the right party propagates narrative ν n in state { ν c } , and the left party propagates narra- tive ν 3 in state { ν c , ν n } . On the equilibrium path, the state changes first to { ν c , ν n } with policy divergence, and then moves to the absorbing state { ν c , ν n , ν 3 } with convergence on deregulation. 2. If δ <  min[ p 2 ( π  − 0 . 5) ,  (1  − p ) p ( π  − 0 . 5)] , in the unique equilibrium, in state { ν c } the right party propagates ν n and the left propagates narrative ν 3 . In state  { ν c , ν n } , the left propagates  ν 3 . On the equilibrium path, the state either 22 changes as in case 1 above, or moves from { ν c } directly to the alternative absorbing state { ν c , ν 3 } , with convergence on deregulation. 3. If (1  − p ) p ( π − 0 . 5) < δ < p 2 ( π − 0 . 5) , in the unique equilibrium, in state { ν c } the right party mixes between propagating ν n and not propagating any new narrative, and the left party mixes between propagating ν 3 and not prop- agating any narrative. In state { ν c , ν n } , the left propagates ν 3 . On the equilibrium path, the state changes as in case 2 above. This proposition clarifies the conditions required for the left to propagate the Third Way narrative preemptively, i.e. before the right deploys the neoliberal nar- rative. It states that the left will propagate the Third Way narrative preemptively only if the narrative communication cost, δ , is relatively low, or if the probability that propagation is successful, p , is relatively high. To understand this, note that the payoff from propagating ν 3 differs from that of propagating ν n since ν n gen- erates an electoral benefit if it succeeds while the Third Way does not (i.e. with probability p (1  − p )), while ν 3 is helpful only if neoliberalism and itself succeeds (with probability p 2 ). Since the success of narratives is probabilistic, these two payoffs are not the same. The left will preemptively propagate the Third Way narrative only if it is not costly to do so (low  δ ), or if narratives deployment is very likely to succeed (high  p ). If instead  δ is high or  p  is low, the left will only propagate the Third Way narrative after neoliberalism has been deployed successfully by the right. Given that the Third Way narrative emerged only some time after the Reagan and Thatcher eras, this seems like the relevant case. Hysteresis? Our results show that temporary economic shocks can have per- manent effects on policy. To see this, observe that if initially we start with  α 0 ≥ α followed by a shock to α 1 ∈ ( α,  ¯ α ) then it is optimal for the right party to prop- agate the neoliberal narrative if condition (17) holds. This leads to a transition to the state { ν c , ν n } and policy divergence. The left party will now counter with the Third Way narrative, leading to the state { ν c , ν n , ν 3 } with a consensus on deregulation. Now even if α reverts to α 0 , this consensus will persist. Thus, a temporary shock leads to a permanent shift in equilibrium policy, via deployment of new narratives. This observation reveals what is special about a model where political dynam- ics are driven by shifts in narratives since, once a narrative is propagated and becomes known to voters, it cannot be withdrawn. This captures the frustration of those who supported the post-war consensus in the UK and US and have been harsh critics of politicians like Clinton, Obama and Blair for their perpetuation of the Third Way which they (correctly) observe preserves many elements of ne- oliberalism. Our explanation for this hysteresis is that, with motivated reasoning, 23 there is no way to persuade the voters to return to the post-war model.

This paper has laid out a framework for studying the role of narratives as subjec- tive causal models in a canonical model of political competition of the kind that dominates the political economy literature, where two parties from the left and right are competing for power and class differences are the key political cleavage. We have explored static and dynamic aspects of a world in which citizens can choose which narratives to incorporate into their beliefs based on self-serving mo- tivated reasoning. This can result in narrative polarization where preferences for divergent policies are driven not just by interests but by differing views about how the economy works. We have argued that this kind of framework can usefully be applied to study the emergence of neoliberal thinking and policy making that emerged in the late 1970s and has become a cornerstone of economic policy ever since. For many commentators, this has led to policy models being sustained that are detached from reality, making some kind of motivated reasoning a plausible explanation. While initiated by an economic shock in the 1970s, its persistence has been a source of puzzlement. As De Long (2023) has put it: “The smart thing to say is that ... neoliberalism is, or ought to be, dead, but zombie-like, it still walks the earth.” But the detachment of narrative-based beliefs from evidence can allow such persistence to emerge and to be sustained. Our approach can also explain why a consensus formed around a particular version of neoliberalism which has been referred to as the “Third Way” since Clinton and Blair. Although these are complex issues, we think that there is value in debating them using a modelling framework which can bring a degree of specificity and pre- cision to what are often quite vague concepts. In spite of the inevitable simplicity of the approach that we have suggested with only two groups, two parties and two policy dimensions, there is richness in the analysis, especially when dynamics are considered. Bringing the theoretical approach to the data is possible using different kinds of text data. First, it is possible to explore shifts in narratives in political speeches in legislatures and elsewhere using new methods that use machine learning to detect causal statements. The framework that we have proposed with three elements to the core narratives can be used to frame the way in which such text data is categorized (Ash et al., 2024). Second, one can look at party manifestos and the influence that neoliberalism has had on policy making through this lens. Another area for further investigation is the nature of narrative innovation. Our core model of eight possible narratives considers each narrative to be a re- 24 combination of existing parameters. Hence the innovation created by the Third Way was a hybrid of the post-war consensus and the neoliberal narrative. Thus, our dynamic model shows the scope for such narratives to have an effect on policy- making. Another way of innovating narratives would be to create new dimensions of policy difference that voters had not been focusing on. These diversification narratives would work by introducing new parameters into the parameter space and bringing attention to aspects of policy that other narratives have ignored. A recent example of such narratives is the way in which arguments about security and resilience are being used to reshape debates about the consequences of eco- nomic openness, an element of neoliberalism. The policy has not changed, only the way that its consequences are being presented to voters. Thus, the new secu- rity and resilience narrative argues for taxes on Chinese imports on cars in the US on the grounds of reducing import dependence on a potentially hostile country. Such narratives have the potential to break out of the Third Way consensus by providing new justifications for low x in our model. We have modeled narratives as being isomorphic to subjective causal models without getting into their cognitive underpinnings. However, exploring these is an important topic for future work on narratives. Arguably what makes a narrative influential is its capacity to make specific causal features of policy salient (Bordalo et al., 2020, 2012) or creating something that is memorable when voters are casting their ballots. Kendall and Charles (2022) study a micro founded model with experimental evidence and it could be fruitful to connect this to policy case studies which could provide a tighter connection to the literature on cognition and bounded rationality in policy choice (Bendor, 2010).
